title: Mesopotamia & Persia
subtitle: Cradles of civilization — Sumer, Assyria, Babylon, and the Achaemenids.
intro: Between the Tigris and Euphrates, writing, law codes, and the first cities. Further east, the Persian empire spread a visual language of winged kings and processional staircases from Egypt to the Indus.

works.title: Warka Vase
works.desc: One of the earliest known narrative reliefs — a procession to the goddess Inanna. Looted from the Iraq Museum in 2003 and later recovered, broken.

works.title: Standard of Ur
works.desc: A wooden box inlaid with lapis, shell, and red limestone. One side shows war; the other, a banquet after the victory.

works.title: Ziggurat of Ur
works.desc: A stepped temple-platform to the moon god Nanna. Its baked-brick facade has been partly restored; the core is as old as the pyramids.

works.title: Code of Hammurabi
works.desc: Diorite stele, seven feet tall. 282 laws in cuneiform — "an eye for an eye" comes from here. Now in the Louvre.

works.title: Lamassu
works.desc: Winged bulls with human heads, guarding palace gates at Nineveh and Nimrud. Carved with five legs so they look correct from front and side.

works.title: Lion Hunt of Ashurbanipal
works.desc: Alabaster reliefs from the king's palace. A dying lioness drags paralyzed hind legs — one of the most unnervingly precise animal studies in ancient art.

works.title: Ishtar Gate
works.desc: Glazed brick gate to Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar II. Dragons and bulls in cobalt blue. Reconstructed in Berlin's Pergamon Museum.

works.title: Cyrus Cylinder
works.desc: A clay cylinder inscribed after Cyrus took Babylon. Often called the first charter of human rights — a claim the British Museum treats with more caution.

works.title: Persepolis
works.desc: Ceremonial capital of Darius and Xerxes. Reliefs show envoys from every corner of the empire bringing tribute. Burned by Alexander in 330 BCE.

works.title: Naqsh-e Rustam
works.meta: Achaemenid & Sasanian · 5th c. BCE – 3rd c. CE · Iran
works.desc: Cruciform tombs cut high into a cliff for Darius the Great and his heirs. Centuries later the Sasanians carved their own triumphs below.

works.title: Mask of Warka
works.desc: The first known naturalistic human face in art — a white marble mask, probably of the goddess Inanna. Looted in 2003, recovered in a farmer's garden.

works.title: Chogha Zanbil
works.desc: The best-preserved ziggurat in the world — five stepped stages of mud brick, built for the god Inshushinak and abandoned when the city fell.

works.title: Behistun Inscription
works.desc: Darius the Great's autobiography carved three hundred feet up a cliff, in three languages. The trilingual key that let Rawlinson decipher cuneiform.

works.title: Tomb of Cyrus at Pasargadae
works.desc: A simple stepped stone tomb for the founder of the empire. Alexander, visiting 200 years later, found it looted and ordered it restored.

works.title: Oxus Treasure
works.desc: 180 gold and silver objects washed out of a riverbed — chariots, lions, votive plaques. The British Museum's greatest hoard of Achaemenid metalwork.

works.title: Naqsh-e Rajab
works.desc: Rock reliefs near Persepolis celebrating the founder of the Sasanian empire. Ardashir receives the crown directly from Ahura Mazda — politics cast as theology.

works.title: Taq Kasra
works.desc: The largest single-span brick vault in the ancient world — the throne hall of the Sasanian palace at Ctesiphon. It still stands, barely.

works.title: Ram in a Thicket
works.meta: Sumerian · c. 2500 BCE · Royal Cemetery of Ur
works.desc: A goat rearing against a golden bush, made of wood, gold leaf, silver, lapis, and shell. Leonard Woolley named it for Abraham's offering in Genesis.

works.title: Apadana Reliefs
works.desc: The great staircase of the audience hall. Subject peoples from 23 nations process forward with tribute — Ethiopians with ivory, Lydians with bowls, Bactrian camels.

works.title: Nimrud
works.desc: Ashurnasirpal II's Northwest Palace, once lined with alabaster reliefs of hunt and war. ISIS dynamited the site in 2015; much had already been moved to museums.

works.title: Babylon
works.meta: Old Babylonian → Neo-Babylonian · 18th c. BCE – 6th c. BCE
works.desc: Capital of Hammurabi, Nebuchadnezzar, and Cyrus. Reconstructed in the 1980s by Saddam Hussein with bricks stamped in his own name.

works.title: Mesopotamian Cylinder Seals
works.desc: Tiny carved stone cylinders rolled across clay to sign a document. Each person owned one — an identity card and an art form at once.

works.title: Statue of Ebih-Il
works.desc: An administrator from Mari, seated with hands folded in prayer, dressed in a tufted skirt. Lapis and shell eyes still stare from a white gypsum face.

works.title: Victory Stele of Naram-Sin
works.desc: The king climbs a mountain over dying enemies, horned helmet marking him as divine. The first ruler in history to have himself depicted as a god.

works.title: Burney Relief
works.desc: A nude winged goddess standing on two lions, flanked by owls. Most likely Ereshkigal, queen of the underworld — or Inanna, descending into it.

works.title: Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III
works.desc: A tribute list in stone — foreign kings bringing gifts to Shalmaneser. One is Jehu of Israel, bowing to kiss the ground. The earliest known image of an Israelite.

works.title: Hatra
works.desc: A desert caravan city whose kings commissioned limestone statues fusing Hellenistic drapery with Parthian dress. Destroyed by ISIS in 2015.

works.title: Stele of the Vultures
works.desc: The earliest known battle monument. King Eannatum's army marches in lockstep behind their shields; on the other side, vultures carry off the heads of the defeated.

works.title: Queen Puabi's Headdress
works.desc: Gold leaves, lapis lazuli pendants, carnelian beads, tied with ribbon hair-wire. Queen Puabi went to her grave wearing this, attended by 23 servants — also buried.

works.title: Royal Cemetery of Ur
works.desc: Sixteen royal graves, each with servants, soldiers, and musicians sacrificed and buried beside the king. Out came the Standard of Ur, Queen Puabi's headdress, and the Ram in the Thicket.

works.title: Yazılıkaya
works.desc: An open-air rock sanctuary near Hattusa. Sixty-six gods and goddesses walk in procession along the cliff face — the full Hittite pantheon gathered in stone.

works.title: Lamassu of Khorsabad
works.meta: c. 713–706 BCE · Gypsum alabaster · Richelieu Wing
works.desc: Human-headed winged bulls, each over 4 meters tall. They guarded the gates of Sargon II's palace in what is now northern Iraq. Carved with five legs so that they appear to be standing still when seen from the front, and striding forward when seen from the side.

works.title: Frieze of the Archers of Darius I
works.meta: c. 510 BCE · Glazed brick · Richelieu Wing
works.desc: From the palace of Darius the Great at Susa. A procession of the king's elite guard — the "Immortals" — in brilliantly colored glazed brick. Each carries a spear, bow, and quiver; each face is subtly different from the next.

works.title: Statue of Gudea
works.desc: More than twenty small diorite statues of the prince of Lagash survive, each showing him at prayer. The diorite was hauled from the Sinai; carving it took tools that barely existed.

works.title: Petra — Al-Khazneh
works.meta: Nabataean · 1st c. BCE – 1st c. CE · Jordan
works.desc: The "Treasury" — a 40-meter temple-facade carved directly into the rose-red sandstone cliff. Bedouin shot at the urn at the top, hoping to spill hidden pharaoh's gold.

works.title: Lyres of Ur
works.desc: Wooden lyres covered in gold and lapis, the sound boxes shaped into bulls' heads. Found in Queen Puabi's grave next to the women who played them. The oldest stringed instruments we have.

works.title: Assyrian Winged Genii
works.desc: Four-winged protective spirits carved in alabaster lining Ashurnasirpal II's palace. They lift a pine cone and a bucket — a gesture of ritual purification never fully decoded.

works.title: Code of Ur-Nammu
works.desc: The oldest known written law code, predating Hammurabi by three centuries. The laws are oddly lenient — mostly fines, only occasional death.

works.title: Ebla
works.meta: Early Bronze Age · c. 2500–1600 BCE · Syria
works.desc: A Syrian city-state whose palace archive held 20,000 cuneiform tablets in a previously unknown Semitic language. Discovered in the 1970s — changed the textbook on the Bronze Age Levant.

works.title: Mari Ziggurat
works.meta: Early Dynastic → Old Babylonian · c. 2500–1750 BCE · Syria
works.desc: A trading capital on the middle Euphrates. Its palace mural of a royal investiture is one of the oldest paintings on plaster to survive.

works.title: Tell Halaf
works.meta: Early Iron Age · 10th c. BCE · Syria
works.desc: Aramean palace built of basalt orthostats — grim, angular statues of gods, scorpions, and lions. Bombed to rubble in Berlin in 1943; a team spent decades reassembling 27,000 fragments.

works.title: Dur-Sharrukin (Khorsabad)
works.desc: The capital Sargon II built from scratch and moved into — only to be killed on campaign and abandoned by his heir. Its 40-ton alabaster lamassus now guard the Louvre and the Oriental Institute.

works.title: Hittite Seated Deity
works.desc: A cast bronze figure in a high conical headdress. The lost Hittite pantheon ran to thousands of names — only a handful survive in objects like this one.

works.title: Kish Tablet
works.desc: A small limestone tablet from Kish covered in proto-cuneiform signs — possibly the earliest known writing. Accounts of barley, fish, and a threshing sled.

works.title: Sumerian Libation Plaque
works.desc: A perforated stone plaque once hung in a temple. The top register shows a naked priest pouring an offering to a seated god; the lower register, worshipers bringing livestock.

works.title: Tell Asmar Hoard
works.meta: Early Dynastic Sumerian · c. 2700 BCE · Eshnunna
works.desc: A cache of twelve alabaster worshiper statues with oversized inlaid eyes of shell and bitumen, designed to gaze eternally at the deity. Found buried beneath the floor of the Square Temple, they stood in for their absent owners in perpetual prayer.

works.title: Tell Brak
works.meta: Uruk-period urban center · c. 4000 BCE · northeast Syria
works.desc: One of the world's earliest cities, older than Uruk itself, dominated by the Eye Temple and thousands of small alabaster 'eye idols' — figurines reduced to enormous staring pupils. A mass grave nearby suggests urban life here could end violently.

works.title: Anubanini Rock Relief
works.meta: Lullubi kingdom · c. 2300 BCE · Kermanshah, Iran
works.desc: The victorious king Anubanini tramples a prostrate foe while the goddess Ishtar delivers captives on a leash. Carved into a vertical cliff, it inspired Darius's later Behistun monument four kilometers away.

works.title: Kul-e Farah Reliefs
works.desc: Six rock-cut panels deep in a southwestern Iranian gorge showing Elamite rulers and banqueters in long processional sacrifices. Little known outside specialist circles, they are the best surviving witness to the visual culture of Elam after the Middle Elamite collapse.

works.title: Temple of Anahita at Kangavar
works.meta: Parthian · 2nd century BCE–2nd century CE · western Iran
works.desc: A vast rectangular terrace edged with columns carved in a hybrid Greco-Persian style, dedicated to the water goddess Anahita. Its scale — more than 200 meters long — and hellenistic detailing testify to the Parthian synthesis after Alexander.

works.title: Taq-e Bostan
works.desc: Rock-cut grottoes carved into a cliff at a sacred spring, showing Sasanian kings receiving divine investiture and hunting boar from reed-filled marshes. The larger grotto's royal boar-hunt panel is among the most elaborate narrative reliefs in Iranian art.

works.title: Bishapur
works.meta: Sasanian royal city · 266 CE · Fars, Iran
works.desc: Built by Shapur I to commemorate his victory over three Roman emperors, including the capture of Valerian. Its grid plan drew on Antioch; its palace mosaics were pure Roman workmanship executed by prisoners-of-war.

works.title: Luristan Bronzes
works.meta: Iron Age nomadic · c. 1200–700 BCE · Zagros Mountains, Iran
works.desc: Thousands of cast-bronze horse bits, standard finials, and pins recovered from shaft graves in western Iran. Animals interlock with humans in tight symmetries that may record forgotten myths of horse-riding pastoralists.

works.title: Marlik Gold
works.meta: Late Bronze Age · c. 1200 BCE · Gilan, Iran
works.desc: A cemetery of about fifty-three princely tombs preserved under mudslide, yielding gold beakers embossed with winged bulls, mythical griffons, and hunting scenes. The humped-bull vessels testify to a local art tradition between the Iranian plateau and the Caspian.

works.title: Ziwiye Hoard
works.desc: A spectacular cache of gold, silver, and ivory found in a bronze coffin in 1947 — or so the story goes; much of it was dispersed on the black market. Surviving pieces hybridize Scythian animal style with Assyrian and Urartian imagery.

works.title: Colossus of Shapur I
works.meta: Sasanian · 3rd century CE · Bishapur cave, Iran
works.desc: A seven-meter standing figure carved from a stalagmite deep in a cave above Bishapur. Shapur stands in full regalia, his thick beard curled and mustaches pointing sideways in the signature Sasanian style; fragments of his arms were reassembled in the twentieth century.

works.title: Eridu Ziggurat
works.meta: Ubaid–Ur III · c. 5400–2100 BCE · southern Iraq
works.desc: Thought to be the oldest city in Mesopotamia. Eighteen superimposed temple layers stand beneath the surviving ziggurat of Ur-Nammu, built over a succession of shrines to the water god Enki stretching back five thousand years.

works.title: Ebla Tablets
works.meta: Early Bronze Age archive · c. 2350 BCE · Syria
works.desc: About 17,000 cuneiform tablets found stacked on collapsed wooden shelves in the burned royal palace of Tell Mardikh. Written in an early Semitic language previously unknown, they revealed that Syria was already a literate, cosmopolitan kingdom a thousand years before Hammurabi.

works.title: Victory Stele of Sargon of Akkad
works.meta: Akkadian · c. 2330 BCE · Iraq (found at Susa)
works.desc: A heavily damaged limestone stele showing Sargon, the world's first empire-builder, leading bound captives while vultures pick at the fallen. Its hierarchical scaling — king monumental, enemies tiny — fixed a template that would outlast Mesopotamia by millennia.

works.title: Relief of Ashurnasirpal II with Attendant
works.desc: Alabaster wall panels from the throne room of Ashurnasirpal's northwest palace, showing the king under a divine winged sun while an attendant purifies him with a fir cone. The minutely patterned beards and tasseled robes remained the template for Assyrian royal art for two centuries.

works.title: Nineveh
works.meta: Neo-Assyrian capital · 7th century BCE · northern Iraq
works.desc: Sennacherib's 'Palace Without Rival' and Ashurbanipal's library stood within twelve kilometers of walls. The Mashki Gate was intentionally dynamited by ISIS in 2016 and has since been partially reconstructed with its original stones.

works.title: Achaemenid Gold Rhyton
works.desc: A drinking horn that ends in the forequarters of a roaring lion, carved from a single piece of gold. Ceremonial, not functional — elite tableware as declaration of empire.

works.title: Dura-Europos Frescoes
works.desc: A caravan-city on the Euphrates whose buried buildings preserved the earliest synagogue murals, the earliest Christian house-church paintings, and Greek, Palmyrene, and Mithraic frescoes — all within blocks of each other.

works.title: Statue of Napir-Asu
works.desc: Headless queen cast in solid bronze, 1.7 tons of metal. An inscription curses anyone who defaces her: may their descendants be destroyed. Threat held — the head, not the name, is gone.

works.title: Head of an Akkadian Ruler
works.desc: Bronze portrait head found buried at Nineveh, likely Sargon or Naram-Sin. Eyes once set with precious stones, gouged out in antiquity as an act of symbolic regicide.

works.title: Lion Gate of Hattusa
works.desc: Two lions carved from the same stone as the gate jambs — one of six ceremonial gates of the Hittite capital. You entered the city through their snarl.

works.title: Yazılıkaya Reliefs
works.desc: An open-air rock sanctuary above Hattusa: two natural chambers with processions of gods carved into the limestone walls. The Hittite pantheon, arranged as if on parade.

works.title: Sphinx Gate of Alaca Höyük
works.desc: A pair of sphinxes guarding the southern entrance to this Hittite sanctuary city, their hair carved into tight curls. Smaller and earlier than the lions at Hattusa — and remained in situ.

works.title: Nimrud Ivories
works.desc: Thousands of carved ivory plaques — Egyptian, Phoenician, and native Assyrian styles — looted from the palace of Kalhu. Many recovered charred from a well; some destroyed by ISIS in 2015.

works.title: Ahiram Sarcophagus
works.desc: Limestone sarcophagus of a king of Byblos. Its inscription is among the oldest substantial texts in the Phoenician alphabet — the writing system that would seed Greek, Latin, and most alphabets since.

works.title: Stele of Ur-Nammu
works.desc: A three-meter limestone stele showing Ur-Nammu ascending to a seated moon-god, a temple being measured and built. Smashed in antiquity; reconstructed fragment by fragment.

works.title: Lachish Reliefs
works.desc: Gypsum slabs from Sennacherib's palace at Nineveh, documenting his siege of the Judean city of Lachish — siege engines, archers, impaled defenders, prisoners marched into exile. Ancient war journalism.

works.title: Pazyryk Carpet
works.desc: The oldest surviving pile carpet in the world, buried in a Scythian chieftain's frozen tomb in the Altai. Horses and deer in rows — nomadic Central Asia meeting Achaemenid design.

works.title: Bactrian Gold
works.meta: Kushan era · 1st c. BCE–1st c. CE · Afghanistan
works.desc: 20,000 gold artefacts from six nomad graves at Tillya Tepe — Greek and Steppe and Indian motifs fused. Thought lost during the Soviet war; reappeared intact from a vault under the Central Bank in 2003.

works.title: Ziwiye Treasure
works.desc: Gold, silver, and ivory found (or said to have been found) in a bronze coffin at Ziwiye in 1947. Scythian and Assyrian styles alongside — how much is authentic, scholars still argue.

works.title: Marlik Golden Cup
works.desc: A gold beaker chased with winged bulls, repeating in a frieze. From a cemetery of tribal chiefs excavated near the Caspian coast — a rare glimpse of a culture that left no writing.

works.title: Tang-e Sarvak Reliefs
works.desc: Rock reliefs in a remote Zagros gorge — kings on horseback, banquet scenes, in a provincial Parthian style that ignores every classical proportion and still somehow works.

works.title: Sasanian Silver Plate
works.desc: Hammered silver with gilded highlights — a king hunting lions, or a noble in ceremonial costume on horseback. Sasanian court plates traveled from Iran as far as Viking Scandinavia.

works.title: Banquet of Ashurbanipal
works.desc: The king reclines in a garden with his queen, a harpist playing — and the severed head of the Elamite king Teumman hangs from a tree branch beside them. Domestic scene as total war.

works.title: Hasanlu Gold Bowl
works.meta: Iron Age · c. 1000 BCE · northwestern Iran
works.desc: A repoussé gold bowl found clutched by three dead men in the ruins of Hasanlu IV, burnt in an attack around 800 BCE. Scenes of gods and chariots — the style pre-Persian, still unnamed.

works.title: Nisa Rhytons
works.desc: Carved ivory drinking horns from the Parthian royal residence at Old Nisa, ending in centaurs, griffins, or winged lions. Greek form, Parthian ritual, Central Asian material.

works.title: Balawat Gates
works.desc: Bronze bands that once wrapped the 7-meter-high wooden doors of Shalmaneser III's palace — embossed with tiny scenes of his campaigns. The wood rotted, the bands survived; now reassembled in the British Museum.

works.title: Statue of Gudea (Met)
works.desc: One of 27 surviving statues of the ruler of Lagash, diorite hauled by camel from the Gulf. The inscription on his lap reads: "Gudea, the man who built the temple, may his life be long."

works.title: Akkadian Head of a Ruler
works.desc: A heavy copper head with a beard in small ribbed curls, eyes once inlaid — one of the earliest large-scale cast-copper portraits anywhere. Nose damaged, no identification certain.

works.title: Achaemenid Lion Rhyton
works.desc: A silver drinking horn terminating in the forequarters of a snarling lion with wings — ceremonial tableware for a Persian noble's banquet, found in the graves of Lydia.

works.title: Bes-Head Necklace
works.desc: A gold necklace of linked beads and a central pendant shaped like the head of Bes — the grotesque Egyptian household god, picked up and recirculated through the Persian empire.

works.title: Sasanian Royal Head
works.desc: A silver-gilt head, perhaps of Shapur II — crown covered in stamped beading, eyes huge and ringed. Sasanian royal portraiture preserved the Achaemenid taste for frontal hierarchy.

works.title: Luristan Horned Animal Plaque
works.desc: A bronze plaque of a stylized horned beast, part of the distinctive animal metalwork of the Luristan tribes — nomadic mountain dwellers with no cities but a rich bronze tradition.

works.title: Luristan Bull Jug
works.desc: A painted ceramic jug shaped as a recumbent bull, spout rising from its back. A domestic vessel that doubled as an animal sculpture — typical of Iron Age Iranian ceramic play.

works.title: Luristan Disc-Headed Pin
works.desc: Bronze boss from a large ceremonial pin — a stylized face or sun with radiating ornament. Luristan pins were votive offerings, deposited by the thousands at shrine sites.

works.title: Ashurnasirpal II Relief Panel
works.desc: A gypsum alabaster relief from Ashurnasirpal's Northwest Palace — the king attended by winged apkallu (sages), standard-topped trees between them. Ritual scene carved as wallpaper.

works.title: Assyrian Ivory Plaque with Oryx
works.desc: An openwork ivory plaque — an oryx grazing in a stylized forest, once set as furniture inlay. Phoenician-carved; looted by Assyrians and buried at Nimrud.

works.title: Phoenician Ivory: Man and Griffin
works.desc: An ivory plaque of a man combating a griffin — once inlaid in royal furniture in Phoenician cities, looted by Assyrians, buried in the wells of Nimrud as Nineveh fell.

works.title: Phoenician Ivory: Cow and Calf
works.desc: A maternal scene carved into ivory — a cow looking back as her calf nurses. Phoenician carvers worked with a Egyptian eye for landscape, an Near Eastern line.

works.title: Phoenician Ivory: Winged Sphinx
works.desc: A crouching winged sphinx ivory plaque — hybrid mythic form that Phoenician artists traded across the ancient Mediterranean. The sphinxes look Egyptian; their wings, Assyrian; their wedding, Phoenician.

works.title: Median Ram-Head Rhyton
works.desc: A silver ram-head drinking horn from Median Iran — predecessors to the Achaemenids. Medes dominated the Zagros before Cyrus the Great absorbed them into a larger empire.

works.title: Urartian Bull-Head Attachment
works.desc: A bronze bull-head fitting — the clamp for a cauldron rim, or the base of a throne leg. Urartu, the lost Iron Age kingdom around Lake Van, specialized in bronze animal protomes.

works.title: Scythian Elk Fawn Finial
works.desc: A small bronze elk-fawn standard top, ears erect — Scythian nomad metalwork from the Pontic steppe. The animal style dominated Scythian visual culture from the Black Sea to Altai.

works.title: Scythian Stag Plaque
works.desc: A cast bronze stag curled in the Scythian "sleeping deer" pose — legs tucked under, antlers sweeping back along the body. A portable ornament, sewn onto leather or horse gear.

works.title: Scythian Lion Plaque
works.desc: A small bronze lion plaque — the "lion in attack" pose, angular and compact. Similar forms span the steppe from Iran to Mongolia in the Iron Age.

works.title: Palmyrene Procession of Nobles
works.desc: A relief of robed Palmyrene nobles in frontal procession — the caravan city brokered silk between Rome and Parthia, before Zenobia's revolt brought Aurelian's legions down on it in 272.

works.title: Parthian Head of a Girl
works.desc: A small terracotta head, broken from a figurine — mass-produced in Parthian workshops between Tigris and Euphrates, where Greek portrait conventions met Iranian faces.

works.title: Hittite Stag Poletop
works.desc: A cast-bronze stag that crowned a wooden standard — the Hittites worshipped a stag-god alongside the storm-god, and metal figurines like this stood on ceremonial carts.

works.title: Hittite Relief Vessel with Ritual Scenes
works.meta: Anatolia · Hittite · 14th–12th c. BCE · burnished earthenware
works.desc: An earthenware jar molded in four panels of Hittite ritual scenes — one of only a handful of figurative narrative vessels from the empire that fought Ramesses II at Kadesh.

works.title: Sasanian Royal Hunt Dish
works.meta: Sasanian Iran · 5th–6th c. CE · silver gilt
works.desc: King Hormizd on horseback twisting to shoot a lion that has leapt onto his saddle. Sasanian hunt dishes travelled as royal diplomatic gifts, reaching as far as the Volga.

works.title: Sasanian Water Buffalo Rhyton
works.meta: Sasanian Iran · 6th–7th c. · gilt silver with glass inlay
works.desc: A drinking horn whose spout is a water buffalo, its rider a woman — likely the goddess Anahita, whose cult fused fertility, water, and war in late Sasanian Iran.

works.title: Sumerian Striding Goat
works.meta: Sumer · c. 3200 BCE · limestone with Egyptian blue inlay
works.desc: A limestone goat with inlaid blue eyes, from the Uruk period just before writing — Sumerian temple workshops fixed the animal forms that would echo through the next three millennia.

works.title: Amlash Lion Hunting Cup
works.desc: A raised silver cup from south of the Caspian, showing a hunter driving his spear into a lion. Amlash metalwork surfaces in tombs along a mountain corridor Iranian-speakers followed south into the plateau.

works.title: Horse-Shaped Drinking Vessel
works.meta: Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 200–325 CE · silver, partially gilt

works.title: Bowl
works.meta: Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 500–700 CE · silver

works.title: Anahita Vessel
works.meta: Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 300–500 CE · silver, cast, raised, repoussé, chased, and gilted

works.title: Stepped Pitcher
works.meta: Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 400–600 CE · silver

works.title: Jar
works.meta: Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 222–650 CE · earthenware

works.title: Ibex Eating
works.meta: Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · c. 400–600 CE · stucco (gypsum plaster) with traces of paint

works.title: Dish: The Goddess Anahita
works.meta: Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 400–600 CE · silver gilt

works.title: Sasanian King Hunting Lions
works.meta: Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · late 300s CE · alabaster

works.title: Fire Altar Intaglio
works.meta: Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE), reign of Hormizd II (303–309) · c. 400 CE · chalcedony

works.title: Feline-Handled Incense Burner
works.meta: Burudjird(?), Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · c. 100 CE · bronze, cast

works.title: Peacock Ring
works.meta: Seleucia, Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · gold and chalcedony

works.title: Pendant Earring
works.meta: Seleucia, Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · gold with garnet and pearls

works.title: Pendant or Earring
works.meta: Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · gold

works.title: Draped Figure
works.meta: Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · terracotta

works.title: Head of a Goddess
works.meta: Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · terracotta

works.title: Head of a Child
works.meta: Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · terracotta

works.title: Vase
works.meta: Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · terracotta

works.title: Figurine
works.meta: Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · bone

works.title: Head of a Weeping Woman
works.meta: Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · terracotta

works.title: Garnet
works.meta: Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · gold and garnet

works.title: Horse Bit with Winged Ibex Cheekpieces
works.meta: Iran, Luristan · 800–600 BCE · bronze, cast and incised

works.title: Master of Animals Cheekpiece
works.meta: Iran, Luristan · c. 700 BCE · bronze, cast and incised

works.title: Votive Pin with Decorated Disc
works.meta: Iran, Luristan · 800–600 BCE · silver, repoussé and incised

works.title: Axel Shaft Cup
